d2609ce6-fbb1-4d53-b2fc-a1c6adbff681.titulo: Sound as a Wave: Physics Applied to Live Audio Engineering
d2609ce6-fbb1-4d53-b2fc-a1c6adbff681.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Before touching a single cable or console, the professional operator needs to understand what they are manipulating: pressure waves in the air. This lesson builds the physical foundation that underpins every technical decision in the course. Without it, EQ, gain and miking become trial and error.

Sound is a variation in pressure that propagates through an elastic medium (usually air) and that our ear interprets as pitch, intensity and timbre. Mastering frequency, amplitude, wavelength, phase and the dB relationship is what separates the operator who 'tweaks until it sounds good' from the one who knows what they are doing.

  • Frequency and pitch: Frequency is the number of cycles per second, measured in hertz (Hz). The human audible spectrum runs from roughly 20 Hz to 20 kHz, and in practice speech intelligibility is concentrated between 300 Hz and 3.4 kHz. Lows (20–250 Hz), mids (250 Hz–4 kHz) and highs (4–20 kHz) are the 'colors' of sound that the equalizer manipulates. Knowing where each instrument lives in the spectrum (kick ~60 Hz, vocal body ~200 Hz, vocal presence ~3–5 kHz, cymbal sheen ~10 kHz) is a prerequisite for any conscious EQ.
  • Amplitude and the decibel (dB): Amplitude is the 'strength' of the wave and we perceive it as loudness. Because the ear responds logarithmically, we use the decibel. Note: the dB is a ratio, not an absolute value — it only makes sense with a reference. dB SPL (Sound Pressure Level) measures sound pressure in the air (0 dB SPL = threshold of hearing; ~120 dB SPL = threshold of pain; a rock show can exceed 100 dB SPL in the audience). dBu and dBV measure electrical signal voltage. dBFS (Full Scale) measures the digital level, where 0 dBFS is the absolute ceiling — above it there is irreversible clipping. Confusing these scales is a classic beginner mistake.
  • Wavelength and why bass is 'difficult': Wavelength (λ) = speed of sound (~343 m/s in air at 20 °C) divided by frequency. A 40 Hz low has λ ≈ 8.5 m; a 10 kHz high has λ ≈ 3.4 cm. This explains practical phenomena: lows bend around obstacles and 'fill' the room (they are hard to steer and tame), while highs are directional and easily absorbed/blocked. It is the reason subwoofers don't need precise aiming and high-frequency drivers must point at the audience.
  • Phase and cancellation: Phase is the position of the wave within its cycle, measured in degrees (0–360°). When two copies of the same signal arrive 180° out of phase, they cancel (lows disappear, the sound becomes thin and 'hollow'). This is the silent enemy of miking with several microphones and of using a DI + microphone simultaneously on the same instrument. The practical '3:1' rule (the second microphone should be at least 3× farther from the source than the first) is born from phase control.
  • Timbre and harmonics: Two instruments playing the same note sound different because of timbre, which is the set of harmonics (whole-number multiples of the fundamental frequency) and their dynamic envelope (attack, sustain, decay). Understanding harmonics explains why cutting the fundamental doesn't always 'kill' the note and why harmonic exciters and analog saturation 'color' the sound.

Step by step

  1. Calculating a wavelength: Take the target frequency and divide 343 by it. E.g.: to treat a room mode at 80 Hz → 343/80 ≈ 4.3 m. This indicates that thin panels don't absorb that low; you need a thick bass trap.
  2. Identifying bands by ear: Use a tone generator or a parametric EQ 'sweeping' a narrow band with a +10 dB boost over a familiar track. Move the frequency and memorize the feel of each region: the 'kick' at 60–80 Hz, the 'mud' at 200–400 Hz, the 'harshness' at 2–4 kHz, the 'sibilance' at 6–8 kHz.

In practice: At a wedding in a hall with a high ceiling and glass walls, the operator notices the celebrant's voice 'disappearing' and sounding muffled. Instead of just turning up the volume (which causes feedback), they recognize that the hall has long reverberation from reflective surfaces and that intelligibility depends on the 1–4 kHz range. Rather than fighting physics, they position the speakers closer and lower toward the audience and gently boost the vocal presence — a decision that only makes sense to someone who understands waves, frequency and reflection.

Summary

  • Sound is a pressure wave described by frequency (pitch), amplitude (loudness), phase and timbre.
  • The decibel is always a ratio — dB SPL, dBu and dBFS measure different things and are never interchangeable.
  • Wavelength explains why lows are hard to steer/absorb and highs are directional.

Frequency, wavelength and interference

Sound is a wave: frequency (Hz) sets the pitch (low/high) and amplitude sets the loudness. Wavelength λ = speed of sound (343 m/s) ÷ frequency — lows have long waves (meters), highs have short waves (centimeters). When two waves of the same frequency add together, interference occurs: in phase they reinforce (more loudness); at 180° (out of phase) they cancel. That's why two microphones or two subwoofers out of phase lose bass — phase cancellation.

See and HEAR it in the simulator below: change the phase and hear the sum vanish.

0b02cbc9-7618-40b6-970b-df9e6b03a129.titulo: The Signal Chain: From Microphone to Loudspeaker
0b02cbc9-7618-40b6-970b-df9e6b03a129.conteudo_rico:
To begin: All live sound travels the same logical path: capture, pre-amplification, processing, mixing, power amplification and transduction back into sound. This lesson presents the 'mental map' the operator uses to diagnose any problem quickly.

The signal chain (signal flow) is the backbone of the profession. When there is no sound, or it distorts or hisses, the competent operator doesn't panic: they walk the chain point by point. Understanding signal levels (mic, line, instrument, speaker) and where each stage happens is what makes troubleshooting fast.

  • The four signal levels: Microphone signal (mic level): very weak, on the order of millivolts, requires pre-amplification. Instrument signal (instrument/Hi-Z): from guitar/bass pickups, high impedance, also weak. Line signal (line level): the standard working level inside the console and between equipment (+4 dBu professional, −10 dBV consumer). Loudspeaker signal (speaker level): already power-amplified, high voltages and currents that move the cone. Connecting the wrong levels (e.g. speaker level into a line input) damages equipment.
  • The complete path: Source (voice, instrument) → input transducer (microphone/DI) → cable → preamplifier (gain) → A/D converter (in digital consoles) → channel processing (EQ, gate, compressor) → routing (bus/aux/group) → summing on the master → D/A converter → system processing → power amplifier → loudspeaker → sound in the air → the audience's ear. Every arrow is a possible point of failure.
  • Transduction: the essence of audio: A transducer is any device that converts energy from one domain into another. The microphone converts acoustic pressure into electrical voltage; the loudspeaker does the reverse. The entire chain is, in fact, the careful manipulation of the electrical signal between these two transducers. The better each conversion, the more faithful the result.
  • Gain staging as a key concept: Each stage has an ideal operating range: a signal that is too high distorts (clipping); too low brings noise when amplified later (poor signal-to-noise ratio). Gain staging is the adjustment of the correct level at each point in the chain to keep the signal strong and clean from beginning to end. It will be detailed in Module 3, but it starts to be understood here.
  • Analog vs. digital in the flow: In the analog world the signal remains a continuous voltage from start to finish. In digital, the signal is converted into numbers (A/D) right after the pre, processed mathematically and reconverted (D/A) at the output. This changes where adjustments happen (on the screen/DSP instead of circuits), but the logical map of the chain is the same.

Equipment / details

  • DI box (direct injection box) — e.g. BSS AR-133, Radial ProDI/J48, Behringer DI100: Converts a high-impedance instrument signal (bass, acoustic guitar with pickup, keyboard) into a balanced, low-impedance signal for the console's microphone input. Active ones (needing phantom/battery) give more headroom for active pickups; passive ones are robust and simple. Essential for bass and keyboards in the PA.
  • Multicore / Snake and digital Stage Box: The analog multicore carries dozens of channels from the stage to the console in a single bundle (e.g. 24 channels + returns). In digital systems, the stage box (e.g. Midas DL16/DL32, Behringer S16) sits on the stage, performs the A/D conversion there and transmits everything over a single network cable (Cat5e/AES50), reducing weight, noise and losses over long distances.

Step by step

  1. Troubleshooting 'no sound' by walking the chain: 1) Is the source producing sound? 2) Is the cable good and connected? 3) Is phantom power on if it's a condenser? 4) Is the gain open and the channel unmuted? 5) Are the channel and bus/master faders above −∞? 6) Is the channel correctly routed to the master? 7) Is the amplifier on and the speaker connected? Testing point by point avoids the panic of touching everything at once.
  2. Identifying the level of each connection before plugging in: Before connecting, classify it: is it mic, instrument, line or speaker? Channel XLR input = mic/line; 1/4" input with a Hi-Z switch = instrument; amplifier output to a passive speaker = speaker (never into a console). This check prevents damage.

In practice: At a band show, the bassist complains that the sound is 'faint and muffled' out front. The operator walks the chain: they discover the bass was plugged directly into a line input without a DI, causing an impedance mismatch and loss of highs and level. They insert an active DI, the signal regains body and definition, and the gain becomes stable. Without the mental map of the signal chain, they would have wasted time tweaking EQ without solving the real cause.

Summary

  • The signal chain goes from source to audience through capture, pre, processing, mixing and power.
  • There are four signal levels (mic, instrument, line, speaker) that cannot be mixed without care.
  • Diagnosing problems means walking the chain point by point, not tweaking everything at random.

29095f5e-90f3-473a-890e-469cb7d8fa87.titulo: Cables, Connectors and Connections: Balanced vs. Unbalanced
29095f5e-90f3-473a-890e-469cb7d8fa87.conteudo_rico:
To begin: A bad cable or one wired wrong is the #1 cause of hiss, signal drops and headaches at events. This lesson turns the operator into someone who chooses, identifies and diagnoses connections with confidence.

In sound systems, different cables carry analog, digital, power and mains signals. Understanding the difference between balanced and unbalanced, mastering the connectors on the market and knowing how to choose by distance and shielding is daily operational knowledge. This lesson stays faithful to the base material and enriches it with the engineering behind each choice.

  • Why balanced beats noise: An unbalanced cable (TS, RCA) has 2 conductors: signal and shield/ground. Over long distances it picks up interference (60 Hz hum, RF). A balanced cable (XLR, TRS) has 3 conductors: hot signal, cold signal (the same information inverted) and shield. At the receiver, the circuit subtracts cold from hot: the signal adds up and doubles, but noise picked up equally on both cancels out (common-mode rejection). That's why microphones and long runs use balanced XLR/TRS.
  • Everyday analog cables: RCA: home stereo, players, some mixers (white/red). 3.5 mm (mini-jack): headphones, phones, laptops; mono or stereo. 1/4" (6.35 mm): instruments, amplifiers, consoles; TS (mono/unbalanced) and TRS (balanced or stereo) versions. XLR (cannon): the professional standard for microphones and balanced connections, robust and lockable. TS vs. TRS: TS (Tip-Sleeve) is mono/unbalanced for instruments; TRS (Tip-Ring-Sleeve) is balanced or stereo and reduces interference.
  • Digital cables: Optical Toslink: optical fiber, digital audio immune to electrical interference. Coaxial digital (S/PDIF): uses an RCA connector but transmits digital over copper. AES/EBU: professional balanced digital, usually on XLR. USB: audio interfaces, MIDI controllers. HDMI: audio+video in home theater. MIDI: transmits control data (notes, commands) between electronic instruments — it is not audio.
  • Power and mains cables: Speaker cable: connects the amplifier to a passive speaker; two thick, low-resistance conductors to carry high power (never confuse it with an instrument cable). Speakon (Neutrik's locking connector): the safe professional standard for high power, replacing the 1/4" jack in PA. PowerCON: a locking power connector for stage equipment. IEC: the standard mains cable of amplifiers, consoles and power supplies.
  • Selection criteria: Distance: prefer balanced (XLR/TRS) on long runs. Impedance: match the equipment. Conductor quality: pure copper (OFC) conducts better and lasts longer. Shielding: a dense braid rejects noise and RF. Connectors: prefer reliable brands (Neutrik) with good strain relief — most failures occur at the connector's solder joint, not in the middle of the cable.

Equipment / details

  • XLR connector (Neutrik X series): 3 pins (1=ground/shield, 2=hot, 3=cold). The worldwide standard for microphones and balanced lines. A safety latch prevents accidental disconnection. Use it for every microphone and every long balanced connection.
  • Speakon connector (Neutrik NL4): A locking power connector to connect an amplifier to a passive speaker. Handles high current safely, with no shock risk when handling. It replaces the 1/4" jack in professional systems; never confuse it with a line signal.
  • Balanced 1/4" TRS cable: 1/4" with 3 contacts (tip, ring, sleeve). Used for balanced outputs of interfaces, inserts and monitor connections. It reduces noise compared to TS. Note: the same connector can be stereo (headphones) or balanced mono (line) — knowing the context avoids mistakes.

Step by step

  1. Testing a suspect cable: Use a cable tester (e.g. Behringer CT100) that shows continuity pin by pin, or plug it into a known input and flex the cable near the connectors, listening for hiss/drops. Identify whether the fault is at the plug (most common) and resolder or discard it.
  2. Planning an installation by distance: Map each run: microphone on stage to the console (balanced XLR, can be long); console output to amplifiers (balanced XLR/TRS); amplifier to speakers (speakon, short and thick run). Avoid running audio parallel to power cables; when they cross, do it at 90°.
  3. Organizing and coiling cables (over-under technique): Coil by alternating one normal loop and one reversed loop (over-under) so the cable doesn't 'memorize' twists and lasts longer. Label each cable with tape/a tag. A well-cared-for cable means reliability at the next event.

In practice: At a corporate event, the video screen starts injecting a low hum into the audio every time it turns on. The operator identifies a ground loop aggravated by a long unbalanced cable between the laptop and the console. They swap the connection for a DI with a ground lift and a balanced XLR cable; the hum disappears. The solution came from understanding that balanced rejects noise and that ground loops are solved by isolating, not by raising the volume.

Summary

  • A balanced cable (3 conductors) cancels noise through common-mode rejection; unbalanced picks up interference on long runs.
  • Each connector has a purpose: XLR for mic/line, Speakon for power, 1/4" TS/TRS for instrument/balanced, IEC/PowerCON for mains.
  • Most failures are at the connector — testing, resoldering and organizing cables is essential maintenance.

Finishing: cable routing and organization

A professional never leaves cables tangled. Rules: protect runs across audience areas (cable ramp/gaffer tape); cross signal vs. power at 90° (never parallel together); coil the slack in over-under with velcro; label everything; run along the edges and back, out of the walkways.

Practice in the simulator below.

9c0afa92-a872-4049-a1c2-33bf6d9bc725.titulo: Mixing Consoles: Analog, Digital and Rack
9c0afa92-a872-4049-a1c2-33bf6d9bc725.conteudo_rico:
To begin: The console is the brain of the operation. This lesson presents the universal anatomy of a console and compares the three families — analog, digital and rack digital — with the real models the operator encounters on the Brazilian market.

Sound console, mixing console or mixer: the equipment that combines, processes and routes all sources. Knowing the anatomy (channels, pre, EQ, dynamics, aux/bus, master) is universal; knowing the advantages of each family and the reference models is what prepares you for the real market.

  • Universal console anatomy: Regardless of brand, every console has: input channels (with an XLR/TRS connector, preamplifier and gain), an EQ section per channel, faders and pan, auxiliary sends (aux), mute/solo buttons, subgroups, a master section with the main fader and meters, and an output section (master, monitors, effects). In digital all of this exists, but it is multiplexed across layers and screens — the same physical fader controls different channels depending on the selected layer.
  • Analog: advantages and limits: Analog consoles keep the signal as a continuous voltage. Advantages: immediate workflow (one knob = one function, always visible), zero latency, and the pleasant 'color' and saturation of good pres and tape. Limits: they don't store scenes, they take up space, and they require outboard (external processors) for effects and dynamics. Still used at small events, churches and as a learning tool because of the transparency of the routing.
  • Digital: why it took over the live market: Digital consoles convert the signal and process everything in DSP. Decisive advantages: storing and recalling scenes/snapshots (essential at repetitive events and festivals with fast band changeovers), parametric EQ, gate and compressor on every channel without outboard, internal effects, flexible routing, remote control by tablet, and portability. It is now the standard at most professional events.
  • Rack console: A mixer in rack format (19 inches) with no (or few) physical controls, operated by software/tablet (e.g. Behringer X32 Rack, Midas M32R/M32C, Allen & Heath SQ in compact format). Advantage: maximum portability and less weight; ideal for stages where the console stays on the stage and the operator controls it over Wi-Fi/tablet, or for fixed installations. The same DSP power in a smaller cabinet.
  • Layers, banks and scenes in digital: Since there are more channels than physical faders, digital uses layers/banks: the row of faders 'rolls' between groups of channels (1–16, 17–32, buses, DCAs). Scenes or snapshots save the console's complete state (gains, EQ, faders, mutes, routing) for instant recall. Mastering layers and scenes is the central skill of the modern digital operator.

Equipment / details

  • Behringer X32 / Midas M32: The entry standard on the Brazilian professional market. 32 input channels, 16 mix buses, 8 DCAs, internal effects, Midas preamplifiers (on the M32, identical to those of larger consoles). Control via app (X32-Edit / M32-Edit / Mixing Station). The value for money that made it ubiquitous in churches, bands and production companies. The M32 is the Midas version with premium pres and build.
  • Yamaha CL5 / QL5: A line of highly reliable digital consoles widely used at large events, theaters and broadcast. Remote preamplifiers via the Dante network, premium processing (Rupert Neve effects via the Premium Rack), and a mature, stable interface. The CL5 has more faders; the QL is more compact. A benchmark for robustness at large-scale events.
  • Allen & Heath SQ / dLive: SQ (SQ-5/SQ-6/SQ-7): a compact line with 96 kHz processing, low latency and an excellent dynamics/EQ section. dLive is the flagship for large productions, with a separate surface and MixRack. Highly appreciated for sound quality and workflow. Common in large churches and tours.

Step by step

  1. Recognizing the anatomy when arriving at a new console: Locate: inputs on the rear/side panel, the gain knob, the phantom button (+48V), the EQ section, the channel fader, mute/solo buttons, aux/effect sends, bus/group faders, the master fader and the meters. Even different models follow this logic.
  2. Navigating between layers on a digital console: Use the layer/bank buttons to switch between channels 1–16, 17–32, buses and DCAs. Select a channel (Select button) so the screen shows its complete channel strip. Practice until navigation becomes automatic — during a show there's no time to search.
  3. Saving and recalling a scene: After building the mix, save a named scene (Scene/Snapshot) (e.g. 'Band A — show'). For the next band, recall another scene or a clean 'template'. Always check what the scene recalls (some parameters may be 'safe'/protected, like stage gain).
  4. Consulting the manufacturer's manual for specific paths: The standard flow (gain, EQ, dynamics, send, fader, master) is universal, but the exact menu path varies by model. Keep the console's manual/quick-start handy and, when possible, train on the offline software (X32-Edit, etc.) before the event.

In practice: At a festival with five bands and 15-minute changeovers, the operator uses an X32: for each band there's a scene saved at rehearsal with gains, EQ and routing. At the changeover, they simply recall the correct scene and adjust fine details. Without scene storage — impossible on an analog console — these lightning-fast changeovers wouldn't be viable. It's the perfect example of digital's advantage in live sound.

Summary

  • Every console shares the same anatomy; digital organizes it into layers, screens and scenes.
  • Digital took over live sound because it stores scenes and offers dynamics/EQ/effects on every channel.
  • Reference models: Behringer X32/Midas M32 (pro entry), Yamaha CL/QL (large scale), A&H SQ/dLive.

7561ee37-b303-474b-bf3d-24f0310e52aa.titulo: Microphones: Types, Polar Patterns and Reference Models
7561ee37-b303-474b-bf3d-24f0310e52aa.conteudo_rico:
To begin: The microphone is the first link in the chain — and the best mix can't fix a bad capture. This lesson masters the transduction types, polar patterns and the real models the operator instinctively chooses in the field.

Dynamic, condenser and ribbon; directional and omnidirectional. Each microphone is a tool with the right application. Knowing the physical principle, the polar pattern and the reference models (Shure, Sennheiser, AKG) is indispensable market knowledge.

  • Dynamic microphones: They use a moving coil in a magnetic field: the diaphragm's vibration generates current. Rugged, they handle very high sound pressure (SPL), don't need phantom power, and are less sensitive to subtle detail. Ideal for the stage: live vocals, guitar amplifiers, snare and kick drum. The default choice where durability and toughness matter.
  • Condenser microphones: A thin diaphragm in front of a charged plate; variations in capacitance generate the signal. Very sensitive, with a wide, flat frequency response, they capture detail and brightness. They require external power (phantom power +48V). Less tolerant of extreme SPL. The studio standard for detailed vocals and acoustic instruments; live they appear as drum overheads, choir capture and acoustic guitar.
  • ['Ribbon microphones', "A metal ribbon suspended in a magnetic field; a smooth, natural, 'vintage' sound, with a somewhat more limited response at the extreme highs. Sensitive and fragile, they require careful handling and caution with phantom on old models. More common in the studio (brass, strings, mellow guitar). Knowing they exist and when they show up", 'keeps the operator complete.']
  • Polar patterns: They define where the microphone captures from. Omnidirectional: captures 360°, natural sound, great for ambience and balanced capture, but bleeds everything (bad on a noisy stage). Cardioid: captures from the front, rejects the back — the most-used pattern on stage to isolate the source. Supercardioid/hypercardioid: more directional, with a narrower side rejection, great against feedback but requiring precise aiming. Figure-8 (bidirectional): captures front and back, typical of ribbon microphones and stereo techniques.
  • Proximity effect and off-axis: Directional microphones boost lows when very close to the source (proximity effect) — the vocalist who 'eats' the microphone becomes more bass-heavy/full. Sound captured off-axis loses highs and changes timbre; that's why the microphone's aim matters as much as the choice of model.

Equipment / details

  • Shure SM58 and SM57: SM58: dynamic cardioid, the worldwide standard for live vocals, with a pop-filter grille and a response shaped for the voice; practically indestructible. SM57: the same element without the grille, the most versatile microphone in the world — snare drum, guitar amplifier, brass, percussion. An essential pair on any rider.
  • Shure Beta 52A and AKG D112 (kick drum): Dynamics designed for kick drum and high-SPL lows, with a boosted low-end response and presence for the beater's 'click'. The Beta 52A (supercardioid) and D112 are references for capturing kick with weight and definition live.
  • Sennheiser e604 / e835 and Shure SM7B / Beta 58A: Sennheiser e604: a clip-on dynamic for toms and snare, handy on the drum kit. e835: a dynamic vocal alternative to the SM58. Shure SM7B: a studio/broadcast dynamic, a benchmark for voice-over, podcasting and full-bodied vocals. Beta 58A: supercardioid, a higher-rejection, brighter version of the SM58 for vocals. AKG C414/C451: studio and overhead condensers.

Step by step

  1. Choosing the microphone by the source: Live vocals: dynamic cardioid (SM58/e835/Beta 58A). Guitar amplifier: SM57. Kick drum: Beta 52A/D112. Snare: SM57/e604. Toms: e604. Overheads and acoustic guitar: small condenser (C451/AT2021). Delicate voice/instrument in the studio: large-diaphragm condenser (C414, U87). The rule: dynamic for high SPL and stage; condenser for detail and studio.
  2. Turning on phantom power safely: Only turn on +48V for condensers (and active DIs that require it). Before turning phantom on/off, leave the channel muted and the fader down to avoid a loud 'pop' that can damage speakers. Dynamic microphones tolerate phantom, but old ribbon microphones can be damaged — always check.
  3. Positioning while respecting the polar pattern: Aim the microphone's main axis (front) at the source and the 'dead' part (the back, in cardioid) at the monitors/speakers, reducing feedback. On a supercardioid, remember that the point of maximum rejection is at the rear sides, not exactly behind.

In practice: At a service, the choir sounds weak and feeds back when captured by vocal dynamics. The operator swaps them for two cardioid condensers on tall stands, using a distant-capture technique, with the rear pointed at the monitors. The sound gains naturalness and brightness and the feedback disappears — the result of matching the transduction type (condenser, sensitive) and polar pattern (well-aimed cardioid) to the situation.

Summary

  • Dynamic for stage and high SPL; condenser for detail and studio; ribbon for a smooth, vintage sound.
  • The polar pattern (omni, cardioid, super/hyper, figure-8) decides what the microphone captures and rejects.
  • Reference models solve most situations: SM58 (voice), SM57 (everything), Beta 52A/D112 (kick).

b18dddf4-56eb-48d7-a86d-baaa44bb3267.titulo: Loudspeakers, Line Array and Power Amplification
b18dddf4-56eb-48d7-a86d-baaa44bb3267.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Sound that doesn't reach the audience well doesn't exist. This lesson covers the output transducers — speakers, line arrays, subwoofers and monitors — and the power amplification that drives them, along with the amplifier classes and impedance precautions.

The PA (Public Address) system is what delivers the sound to the audience. Understanding speaker types, line array systems, subwoofers, amplification and the amplifier classes (with an emphasis on Class D) is the knowledge that defines the system's coverage, power and safety.

  • Active vs. passive speakers: A passive speaker needs an external amplifier and a power cable (speakon); the crossover is internal and passive. An active speaker has the amplifier and processing built in — it just needs a line signal and power. Active ones (e.g. the JBL EON/PRX, QSC K/K.2, RCF ART, Yamaha DBR/DXR lines) dominate small and medium events for their convenience; passive + external amplifier gives flexibility in larger systems.
  • Components of a speaker: Woofer (low/low-mid), compression driver + horn (highs) and, in full-range systems, a crossover that splits the frequencies to each component. The horn's coverage angle (e.g. 90°x60°) defines the dispersion. Knowing how to read specifications (RMS power, maximum SPL, frequency response, dispersion) is part of the technical choice.
  • Line array: why it exists: A line array is a set of small speakers aligned vertically that behave like a single long source. Advantages: long, uniform coverage (the level drops less with distance than with point-source speakers), vertical control of dispersion and less energy toward the ceiling/floor. It is the standard for medium and large events (e.g. the RCF HDL, d&b, L-Acoustics, JBL VTX lines). The array's curvature is calculated to cover from the back to the front of the audience.
  • Subwoofers and configurations: Subwoofers reproduce the sub-bass (~30–100 Hz). They can be configured in cardioid (to reduce bass on stage), endfire or arc to shape the dispersion. Time alignment between the sub and the tops (full-range speakers) avoids cancellations in the crossover region. A well-adjusted subwoofer gives weight without 'muddying' the mix.
  • Amplifier classes: Class A: conducts the entire cycle, high fidelity, very low efficiency (runs very hot) — hi-fi use. Class B: each transistor conducts half a cycle, more efficient but with crossover distortion. Class AB: combines A and B, a good fidelity/efficiency balance — the classic in audio amplification. Class D: uses pulse-width modulation (PWM) switching fast, very high efficiency, light and cool — it dominates the modern PA and active speakers. Classes C/E/F/G/H are specialized (RF and efficiency optimizations).

Equipment / details

  • Active speakers QSC K.2 / RCF ART / Yamaha DXR: Full-range active speakers with Class D amplification and internal DSP (presets, limiter, delay). Handy for small and medium events, as a stage monitor or main PA. Typical specs: 1000–2000 W peak, 12" or 15" + compression driver, SPL of 130+ dB. To hook up, all you need is a line signal + power.
  • Line array system (e.g. RCF HDL / L-Acoustics / d&b): A vertical array of identical modules flown or in a ground stack, with dedicated subwoofers. Used when the audience is large/deep and requires uniform coverage. The configuration (number of speakers, angles, delay) is calculated in the manufacturer's prediction software. This is advanced operator knowledge, but it's what you find at festivals.
  • Class D power amplifier (e.g. QSC, Lab Gruppen, Crown): Light, efficient amplifiers that power passive speakers and arrays. They include DSP, limiters and presets per speaker model. Critical attention to impedance: connecting speakers below the minimum impedance (e.g. 2 Ω on an amp rated for 4 Ω) overloads and can burn out the amplifier.

Step by step

  1. Matching amplifier and speaker (impedance and power): Check the speaker's impedance (4/8/16 Ω) and add them in parallel if there are several. Don't go below the amplifier's minimum impedance. For power, a rule of thumb is for the amplifier to deliver about 1.5 to 2× the speaker's RMS power, using a limiter for protection — this gives headroom without distortion, but always consult the manufacturer's recommendations.
  2. Positioning the PA for coverage: Raise and angle the speakers to cover the audience, not the ceiling. On point-source speakers, aim the horn's axis at the most distant audience. Avoid pointing the PA directly at open microphones on stage (feedback). For a line array, follow the manufacturer software's angle calculation.
  3. Powering up and down in the right order: Turn on the sources and console first, and the amplifiers/active speakers last (with volumes low). To power down, reverse it: amplifiers/speakers first, then the console. This avoids the transient 'pop' that can damage drivers.

In practice: At a party in a deep warehouse with point-source speakers, the audience at the back complains the sound is weak and those at the front that it's too loud. The operator understands that a point-source speaker loses level quickly with distance (inverse-square law) and that the case called for a line array or delay speakers (reinforcement) in the middle of the hall. Without dangerously raising the front volume, they install time-aligned delay speakers, equalizing the coverage — a solution born from understanding propagation and systems.

Summary

  • Active speakers have amplification and DSP built in; passive ones require an external amplifier and speakon.
  • Line array delivers long, uniform coverage; well-aligned subwoofers give weight without confusion.
  • Class D dominates the modern PA because it's efficient, light and cool; always respect impedance and power.

8d217cf1-b4c7-4d09-8cf7-2bd8b88d9cd7.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Between the console and the speakers — and all around the system — there is equipment that ensures quality, conversion and electrical safety. This lesson covers system processors, interfaces/converters, power consumption planning and the RTA analyzer.

The complete operator doesn't stop at the console: they need to understand the system management processor, audio interfaces (A/D-D/A converters), the electrical consumption calculation of the rig, and reading an RTA to equalize the room. These are skills that separate the amateur from the professional.

  • System management processor: Equipment (or DSP) between the console and the amplifiers that performs active crossover (splitting frequencies between sub and tops), system EQ, protection limiting, delay (time alignment) and zone routing. Examples: dbx DriveRack, Behringer DCX2496, processors built into amplifiers. It is what protects the speakers and shapes the PA's sound for the room.
  • Audio interfaces and converters: The interface is the intermediary between the analog and digital worlds. It contains an ADC (analog→digital), a DAC (digital→analog), microphone preamplifiers, gain control and direct (zero-latency) monitoring. Typical connections: XLR, TRS, USB/Thunderbolt. The quality of the converters and pres defines the fidelity of the capture and playback.
  • Rack console vs. interface vs. digital console: These are close but distinct concepts: the interface connects audio to the computer (recording/playback); the digital console mixes live; many digital consoles also work as a multichannel USB interface to record the show (multitrack) and do a virtual soundcheck. Understanding this convergence broadens what the operator can offer.
  • Power consumption planning: Each piece of equipment consumes power (W) or VA. The operator adds up the consumptions to size the mains and avoid a breaker tripping in the middle of the show. Steps: list the components, note the consumption (label/manual), estimate the usage time, calculate the energy (W × h = Wh; ÷1000 = kWh) and add it up. VA becomes W by multiplying by the power factor (~0.8) when needed. Note whether there is dual-voltage equipment and whether a UPS/stabilizer is needed.
  • RTA — Real Time Analyzer: A real-time analyzer that shows the system's frequency response in the room (frequency on the X-axis, amplitude on the Y-axis). With a measurement microphone, it identifies peaks, dips, resonances and helps equalize the PA for the environment and detect feedback frequencies. It exists in hardware and software (e.g. Smaart, REW, RTA apps); it is a calibration tool, it does not replace the trained ear.

Equipment / details

  • Audio interface Focusrite Scarlett / Universal Audio Apollo / RME Babyface: Scarlett 2i2: the entry standard, good pres and converters, USB. Apollo Twin: a low-latency Thunderbolt connection with analog emulations. RME Babyface Pro FS: a benchmark for converters and driver stability. Choose by the number of inputs/outputs, latency, connection and budget. Used to record shows, virtual soundcheck and production.
  • System processor dbx DriveRack / Behringer DCX2496: Manages the PA: active crossover, system EQ, protection limiter and delay alignment. The DriveRack has auto-EQ wizards with a measurement microphone. It protects the speakers and adapts the sound to the room. Valuable knowledge for those who build systems, not just operate the console.
  • Measurement microphone + RTA software (e.g. miniDSP UMIK-1 + REW/Smaart): A calibrated omnidirectional microphone connected to measurement software that generates the RTA and response curves. Used to calibrate the system, find room modes and problem frequencies. A fine-tuning tool for the PA.

Step by step

  1. Building the rig's consumption sheet: List each piece of equipment and its consumption (e.g. 2 active speakers 100 W, controller 30 W, laptop 60 W, LED lighting 80 W, interface 10 W). Multiply by hours of use for Wh, divide by 1000 for kWh, and add up the total. Check whether the available circuit supports the total current (W ÷ voltage = A) with a margin.
  2. Roughly calibrating the PA with RTA: Play pink noise through the system, position the measurement microphone in the listening area and watch the RTA. Correct the exaggerated resonance peaks with system EQ and avoid 'flattening' everything blindly — the RTA guides, the ear decides. Repeat at a few points in the audience.
  3. Setting up the interface to record the show: Connect the console's direct/USB output to the interface/computer, set the sample rate (44.1/48 kHz) and the resolution (24 bits), check the buffer for adequate latency and test the recording level without clipping (peaks below 0 dBFS).

In practice: Before a show at a sports court, the operator adds up the consumption of the PA, lighting and backline and finds that the 20 A circuit wouldn't handle the peaks. They redistribute the load across two circuits and put the console and laptops on a UPS. During the check, they use the RTA and notice a strong resonance at 250 Hz (the 'mud' of the concrete court); they cut that band slightly on the system EQ and intelligibility improves. Electrical planning and measurement kept the show from stopping and made it sound better.

Summary

  • The system processor protects the speakers and adapts the PA to the room (crossover, EQ, limiter, delay).
  • The interface converts between analog and digital and lets you record the show/virtual soundcheck.
  • Planning electrical consumption avoids drops; the RTA guides the room's equalization together with the ear.

RTA: spectrum analyzer to tune the PA

The RTA (Real Time Analyzer) shows the system's frequency response in the room, measured with a measurement microphone and pink noise. The peaks above the reference are room/PA bumps that you cut on the EQ to make the response flatter. Tools: Smaart software, dbx RTA-M with DriveRack, or RTA apps on a phone. Remember: a phone RTA measures audio, it does not scan a microphone's RF.

Operate in the simulator below: turn on the pink noise, read the response and mark the peaks.

b0f7813e-80c9-4528-9230-1f667b128b12.titulo: The Channel Strip and Gain Staging in Practice
b0f7813e-80c9-4528-9230-1f667b128b12.conteudo_rico:
To begin: The channel strip is the processing sequence of each channel — and gain staging is the discipline that keeps the signal strong and clean from beginning to end. Mastering both is the central operational skill of the course.

Each channel of a console follows an order: input, gain/trim, gate, EQ, compressor, fader, pan and routing. This lesson is the channel strip's user manual and the definitive step-by-step of gain staging, the foundation of all mixing without noise or distortion.

  • The channel strip order: The typical flow per channel: 1) Input (XLR/TRS) and source selection; 2) Preamplifier / Gain (Trim) — adjusts the input level; 3) Phantom (+48V) if needed; 4) High-pass filter (HPF/low cut); 5) Gate / Expander; 6) EQ; 7) Compressor; 8) Inserts/effects via send; 9) Fader (volume in the mix); 10) Pan; 11) Routing to bus/group/master. In digital this chain appears on the screen when you select the channel.
  • Gain (Trim): the most important adjustment: The gain sets the signal level right at the input, before everything. Set too low: the signal is weak and, when amplified later, brings noise (poor signal-to-noise ratio). Too high: it clips the pre and distorts, with no way back. The goal is to place the signal in a healthy range — peaks in the meter's 'green/yellow zone', with headroom (margin) before the red. It is the foundation on which everything else is built.
  • Headroom and signal-to-noise ratio: Headroom is the margin between the signal's average level and the ceiling (0 dBFS in digital). Keeping headroom avoids clipping on unexpected peaks (a scream, a drum accent). Signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) is how much the signal is above the background noise. Good gain staging maximizes S/N and preserves headroom at the same time — a balance that defines clean audio.
  • Fader vs. gain: different roles: A classic beginner mistake: correcting a weak level by raising the fader instead of the gain. The fader adjusts how much the channel appears in the final mix; the gain adjusts the 'raw material' at the input. If the gain is wrong, no fader will fix it. First you set the gain (gain staging), then you mix with the faders.
  • Unity gain and gain along the chain: Unity gain (0 dB) is the point where the stage neither amplifies nor attenuates — it comes in and goes out at the same level. Working with faders near unity (the '0' or 'U' mark) gives room to adjust up and down. Every stage that adds gain (gain, EQ with boost, compressor makeup) must be accounted for so the signal doesn't blow up downstream.

Equipment / details

  • Digital console with an input meter (e.g. X32/M32, A&H SQ): Use the channel's input meter — not the output one — to set the gain. Aim for healthy peaks with headroom. Many consoles have a clip/overload indicator at the top of the channel: it should never light up on a normal signal.
  • Meters: PPM, VU and True Peak: PPM/peak meter shows instantaneous peaks (important in digital so you don't clip). VU shows the perceived average level (loudness). Understanding that a signal can have a moderate VU but high peaks helps you leave headroom. In digital, watch the peak/clip; in analog, the balance between VU and the clip LED.

Step by step

  1. Gain staging channel by channel: 1) Set the fader to unity (0/U) and the gain to minimum. 2) Ask the source to play/sing at the show's loudest volume. 3) Raise the gain until the peaks are in the healthy zone of the input meter, with margin before the clip. 4) Don't use the gain for 'volume in the mix' — that's the fader's job. 5) Repeat for each channel. 6) Only then build the balance with the faders.
  2. Inserting the HPF (high-pass) early: Activate the high-pass filter on channels that have no useful lows (voice ~80–120 Hz, acoustic guitar, cymbals) to remove stage noise, plosives and 'mud'. This cleans the mix even before the EQ and frees up headroom.
  3. Checking the chain when reusing a scene: When recalling a scene from another show, always recheck the gains with the actual source of the day — microphones, people and instruments change. A scene recalled with gain from another event is a common source of distortion or weak sound.

In practice: A vocalist has a weak voice during the check and a strong one in the chorus. The operator who set the gain listening only to the weak voice will have clipping in the chorus. The professional asks for the loudest section of the song, sets the gain with headroom for that peak and uses the compressor to control the dynamics — so the channel never distorts and keeps its presence. The difference between amateur and professional here is all about gain staging.

Summary

  • The channel strip processes each channel in a fixed order starting with the gain.
  • Gain staging is setting the correct level at each stage: strong signal, with headroom, no clipping.
  • Gain adjusts the raw material at the input; the fader adjusts how much the channel appears in the mix.

b76a9243-ba12-4e5b-8108-63d9ea89cd29.titulo: Applied Equalization: Types, Curves and Decisions
b76a9243-ba12-4e5b-8108-63d9ea89cd29.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Equalizing is sculpting the sound in the frequency domain. This lesson turns EQ from 'tweak until you like it' into conscious decisions — fixing problems, creating space for each source and shaping the timbre.

The equalizer adjusts the intensity of each frequency band. Mastering the types (graphic, parametric, shelving, high-pass/low-pass) and the parameters (frequency, Q, gain) is what lets you separate instruments in a dense mix and solve room and timbre problems.

  • Types of EQ: Graphic: fixed bands (10–31 bands, e.g. 1/3 octave), each with a slider; great for system/room EQ and general correction. Parametric: each band has adjustable frequency, gain and Q; the most precise, for sculpting instruments and correcting specific resonances. Semi-parametric: adjustable frequency and gain, fixed Q. Digital consoles include a parametric per channel.
  • Curves and filters: Peaking (bell): boosts/attenuates around a center frequency with a width defined by the Q. Shelving (shelf): boosts/attenuates everything above (high shelf) or below (low shelf) a point — good for 'more brightness' or 'more weight' overall. High-pass (HPF): cuts below the point (removes useless lows). Low-pass (LPF): cuts above the point (softens harshness/background noise).
  • The three parameters: Center frequency: where the adjustment acts. Gain: how much it boosts (+) or attenuates (−). Q (bandwidth): how narrow or wide the adjustment is — high Q = surgical (fixing a pinpoint resonance); low Q = musical/broad (shaping timbre). Cuts tend to use a narrower Q; boosts, a broader and gentler Q.
  • Subtract before adding: Professional principle: prefer cutting problem frequencies to just boosting. Boosting everything adds energy, creates 'mud' and consumes headroom. Cutting the 'mud' (200–400 Hz) from several sources usually clears the mix more than boosting highs. Subtractive EQ cleans; additive EQ should be used sparingly.
  • Creating space (carving) in the mix: In a dense mix, instruments fight for the same range. The technique is to give each source its region: cut the kick where the bass rules and vice versa, open up the vocal highs where the others have no presence. Equalizing while thinking about the whole, not each channel in isolation, is the operator's quality leap.

Equipment / details

  • Parametric channel EQ (in X32/M32, Yamaha, A&H): Typically 4 parametric bands + HPF/LPF per channel. Use the 'sweep and cut' method: with a narrow Q and a boost, sweep the frequency until you find the annoying resonance, then reverse it into a cut. A daily tool for taming voice, guitar and drums.
  • Graphic output/system EQ (e.g. dbx 31-band, internal GEQ): 31 bands (1/3 octave) on the PA or monitor output. Used to correct the room's response and, on monitors, to 'pull' feedback frequencies. Each slider is a fixed band; surgical cuts fight feedback.

Step by step

  1. The 'sweep and cut' method to find a problem: 1) On a parametric band, set a narrow Q and a gain of +6 to +10 dB. 2) Sweep the frequency slowly while listening to the source play. 3) When the 'annoying' resonance jumps out, stop. 4) Reverse the gain into a cut (−3 to −6 dB) and adjust the Q. Result: you removed the problem without stripping the source's character.
  2. Standard vocal EQ (a starting point): HPF ~100 Hz; a small cut in the 'mud' (250–400 Hz); a slight presence boost (3–5 kHz) for intelligibility; brightness/air with a gentle high shelf (10 kHz) if openness is lacking; attenuate sibilance (6–8 kHz) if harsh (or use a de-esser). Always adjusting to the person's real timbre.
  3. EQ while thinking about the whole: Solo the channel to diagnose, but decide with the mix playing. If bass and kick clash, choose who rules the sub (e.g. kick at 60 Hz, bass at 100 Hz) and cut the other in that region. Always re-evaluate in context.

In practice: In a dance band, the rhythm guitar and the voice disappear into each other. The operator notices that both dominate 2–4 kHz. They pull that range back slightly on the guitar and boost it on the voice, and cut the 'mud' at 300 Hz on both. Without touching the volume, the voice becomes intelligible and the guitar keeps its presence without masking — EQ used to create space, not just to 'make it pretty' channel by channel.

Summary

  • EQ has types (graphic, parametric, shelving, HPF/LPF) and three parameters: frequency, gain and Q.
  • Cutting problems usually cleans more than boosting; additive EQ should be sparing.
  • Equalize while thinking about the whole, giving each source its band to avoid masking.

Graphic equalizer and the ring out

The 31-band graphic EQ (1/3 octave) is used to tune the system and, especially, in the monitor ring out: when a monitor feeds back at a frequency, you cut that band to gain volume without squealing. Rule: in the ring out you cut what squeals, you don't boost the rest. Models: dbx 231, Klark Teknik DN360, Behringer Ultragraph.

Practice in the simulator below: kill the feedback by cutting the right bands.

406693d0-cd38-4245-b0c7-15f750f44027.titulo: Dynamics: Compressor, Gate, Limiter and De-esser
406693d0-cd38-4245-b0c7-15f750f44027.conteudo_rico:
To start: Dynamics processors control the difference between loud and soft sounds. This lesson is the complete manual on the compressor, gate, limiter and de-esser — with the parameters, starting values and real-world uses of each one.

Compression tames dynamics and adds consistency; the gate cleans up noise and bleed; the limiter protects against peaks; the de-esser controls sibilance. Mastering threshold, ratio, attack, release, knee and makeup is what makes vocals and drums tight and professional.

  • Compressor: the parameters: Threshold: the level above which the compressor acts. Ratio: how much it reduces above the threshold (4:1 = for every 4 dB above, 1 dB comes out). Attack: how quickly it starts acting after crossing the threshold (fast tames transients; slow lets the attack through). Release: how quickly it stops acting when the signal drops. Knee: smooth (soft) or abrupt (hard) transition around the threshold. Makeup gain: recovers the level lost to compression.
  • What compression actually does: It reduces the difference between peaks and quiet parts, making the signal more even and 'present' in the mix without abrupt swings. On vocals it keeps intelligibility from a whisper to a shout; on drums it adds punch; on bass it steadies the level. Subtle compression 'glues'; overdone it 'chokes' and pumps. The gain reduction metering (how much is being attenuated) guides the adjustment.
  • Gate and expander: Gate (noise gate): cuts/attenuates the signal below the threshold, eliminating noise and bleed in the pauses. Parameters: threshold, attack, hold (keeps it open for a time) and release. Used on toms (closes the mic between hits) and on stage to reduce bleed. An expander is a gentler version (it attenuates instead of cutting).
  • Limiter: A compressor with a very high ratio (∞:1) that sets an absolute ceiling: the signal never exceeds the set level. It protects amplifiers and speakers from dangerous peaks and prevents clipping on the master. In the PA, the system protection limiter is essential safety; on the master, it controls the final peak.
  • De-esser and side-chain compression: De-esser: a compressor that acts only on the sibilance range (6–9 kHz), softening harsh 's' and 'sh' sounds in the voice without dulling it. Side-chain: the compressor is triggered by another signal — the classic 'ducking' where speech automatically lowers the music, or the sub 'breathes' with the kick. Advanced features that expand your control.

Equipment / details

  • Channel compressor (X32/M32, A&H, Yamaha): Every channel has a compressor with threshold, ratio, attack, release, knee and makeup, plus a gain reduction meter. Starting point for vocals: ratio 3:1–4:1, medium attack, automatic release, 3–6 dB of gain reduction on the peaks. Adjust by listening, not just by the numbers.
  • Channel gate and emulated classic processors: Per-channel gates for toms and sources with bleed. Many consoles emulate classics (1176-style, LA-2A, dbx 160 compressors) as built-in plugins — knowing the 'character' of each one (fast and aggressive vs. smooth and optical) helps with the choice.

Step by step

  1. Setting up a vocal compressor from scratch: 1) Ratio 3:1. 2) Lower the threshold until you see 3–6 dB of gain reduction on the strongest peaks. 3) Medium attack (lets the attack of the word through) and a release that 'lets go' before the next phrase. 4) Raise the makeup to match the perceived level to bypass. 5) Compare on/off (A/B): it should sound tighter, not dull.
  2. Setting a gate on a drum tom: 1) Threshold above the bleed (snare/cymbals) but below the tom hit. 2) Fast attack so you don't cut the attack. 3) Short hold so the tom rings out fully. 4) A release that closes smoothly after the decay. Result: the tom mic only opens when it is played.
  3. Protecting the system with a limiter: On the system/master processor, set the limiter to the maximum safe level of the speakers (per the manufacturer). It should not be acting all the time (that indicates excessive volume), only containing dangerous peaks.

In practice: During a sermon, the speaker alternates between whispers and shouts. Without compression, they sometimes disappear, sometimes blast out and cause feedback. The operator applies 4:1 compression with 4–6 dB of reduction on the peaks plus makeup, along with a light de-esser. The voice stays tight and constant throughout the hall, without distorting on the shout or vanishing on the whisper — exactly what compression exists to do.

Summary

  • The compressor controls dynamics via threshold, ratio, attack, release, knee and makeup, making the signal even.
  • The gate cleans up noise/bleed in the pauses and the limiter imposes a ceiling that protects the system.
  • The de-esser tames sibilance and the side-chain enables ducking and triggering by another signal.

0e310149-58be-4629-8038-713a432647d1.titulo: Routing: Aux, Buses, Groups, DCAs and Effects
0e310149-58be-4629-8038-713a432647d1.conteudo_rico:
To start: Routing is how the signal travels inside the console until it becomes a PA mix, a monitor mix or an effect. This lesson masters aux sends, subgroups, DCAs and the use of FX by send — the nervous system of the operation.

Auxiliaries, groups, subgroups, DCAs and effect returns let you shape and direct the audio with flexibility: creating monitor mixes, grouping the drums, processing sets and applying reverb to several channels. Understanding pre/post-fader and send/return is essential.

  • Aux sends and monitor mixes: Each channel can send a portion of its signal to an auxiliary bus. The classic live use: creating independent monitor mixes — each musician hears their own blend (more voice for the singer, more drums for the drummer). It also feeds external processors. Each send level is independent of the channel fader (in pre-fader mode).
  • Pre-fader vs. post-fader: Pre-fader: the send is not affected by the channel fader — essential for monitors, since the musician can't lose their mix when you lower the channel in the PA. Post-fader: the send follows the fader — used for effects, keeping the reverb in proportion to the channel volume in the mix. Choosing wrong makes the monitor vary with the PA mix (a classic problem).
  • Groups and subgroups: They group several channels under a single fader/processing. E.g., summing all the drum mics into one group to control the whole kit with one fader and apply bus compression. Subgroups allow hierarchy (groups within groups) and independent routing to different destinations.
  • DCA / VCA: A DCA (Digitally Controlled Amplifier) controls the volume of several channels with one fader, but — unlike a subgroup — it does not sum the audio into a bus; it just controls the faders remotely. Advantage: grouping vocals or the whole band to raise/lower everything together without reprocessing, keeping the original routing. A central tool of modern live mixing.
  • Effects by send/return: Reverb, delay and modulation live are generally used via send: several channels send (post-fader) to an FX bus; the effect returns on a return channel and is summed into the mix. This way a single reverb serves voice, snare and guitar, with an individual amount per channel — efficient and cohesive. Inserting an effect directly on the channel is less common live.

Equipment / details

  • Internal effects engine (X32/M32: 8 FX racks; A&H; Yamaha Premium Rack): Digital consoles bring reverbs, delays, modulations and even emulations of classic processors. Set them up on FX buses fed by sends. Types useful live: hall/plate reverb for vocals, short room for drums, slap/delay for accents.
  • Monitor system: wedge and in-ear (IEM): Floor monitors (wedges, e.g., wedge-shaped active speakers) or in-ear monitors (IEM, in-ear earphones with a wireless transmitter, e.g., Shure PSM300, Sennheiser). IEM reduces stage volume, improves the PA mix and protects hearing. Each IEM/wedge mix comes from an aux send (often stereo on IEM).

Step by step

  1. Creating a monitor mix (wedge): 1) Select the aux bus assigned to the musician's monitor and set it to pre-fader. 2) In 'sends on faders' mode (if available), use the faders to send each channel to the aux at the amount the musician asks for. 3) Start with what they need most (their own voice/instrument). 4) Avoid piling on everything — less bleed, less feedback.
  2. Setting up reverb by send/return: 1) Choose an FX bus and load a reverb. 2) Set the sends of the desired channels to post-fader. 3) Raise the vocal send for the right amount of space. 4) Adjust the return in the mix. 5) EQ the return (cut the reverb's lows) so it doesn't 'muddy' things.
  3. Grouping with a DCA: Assign all the vocals to one DCA and the drums to another. Now one fader controls each set. During a speech moment, lower the band DCA without touching the individual channels — and bring everything back in its original proportion afterward.

In practice: At a show with a band, the vocalist asks for 'more voice' in the monitor right when the operator needs to lower the voice in the PA for an intimate moment. Because the monitor aux is pre-fader, lowering the channel in the PA doesn't affect the singer's monitor — they keep hearing themselves. At the same time, the voice reverb (post-fader) decreases along with the channel, keeping the proportion. Correct routing solves two problems at once.

Summary

  • Aux sends create monitor mixes and feed effects; pre-fader for monitors, post-fader for FX.
  • Groups/subgroups sum and process sets; DCAs control volumes without summing audio.
  • Live effects are used by send/return, serving several channels with an individual amount and cohesion.

Effects unit: reverb and delay

The effect comes in through an Aux/Send and comes back on a channel (return). On reverb: Pre-Delay (pushes back the initial echo), Decay (room size), HF Damp (dulls the brightness) and Mix. On delay: Time (in ms, synced to the music), Feedback (number of repeats) and Mix. Units: Lexicon, TC Electronic, Yamaha SPX; on digital consoles it comes built in.

Operate it in the simulator below: choose the program and adjust the parameters.

4bf47728-0da7-4320-92c0-e51c4cc2d183.conteudo_rico:
To start: Before the first sound comes the setup: positioning the PA, running cables, defining the input map and checking channel by channel. This lesson is the step-by-step of the operator arriving to an empty room and leaving the system ready to sound.

Professional setup follows order and method: studying the rider/input list, distributing power, positioning speakers, organizing cables, patching inputs and doing the line check. A well-done line check eliminates 80% of the scares during soundcheck.

  • Technical rider and input list: The rider is the document describing the technical needs (PA, microphones, stage, power); the input list (input map) lists what goes into each channel (1-Kick, 2-Snare, 3-Voice...). Reading and setting up from them avoids improvising. The complete operator knows how to interpret and, when needed, create these documents.
  • Power and grounding: Distribute the load across suitable circuits (we covered power draw in Module 2). Keep audio and power separated; use the same ground point for the whole audio system whenever possible, avoiding ground loops (a cause of hum). Never remove the ground pin (a groundless 'cheater' adapter is a dangerous hack).
  • Positioning and cabling: Position the PA covering the audience and off the axis of the microphones (anti-feedback). Run the multicore/stage box to the console, distribute the labeled stage cables, avoid tripping hazards (tape/cable trays). Clean cabling means safety and speed in troubleshooting.
  • Patch and channel map: Connect each microphone/DI to the correct input per the input list and confirm on the console that the channel matches. Name and (on digital) color-code the channels. An organized patch is what lets you find 'channel 7 is the snare' instantly during the show.
  • Line check: Before the musical sound, check each channel: request/generate a signal at the source and verify that the right channel receives it, without noise, with phantom where needed. Check the polarity/phase of pairs (overheads, DI+mic). The line check validates all the infrastructure before the band arrives.

Equipment / details

  • Stage box / multicore and labeling system: A digital stage box or analog multicore to bring the channels to the console. Labels, colored tape and numbering on the cables. A flashlight/headlamp to work on a dark stage. Physical organization is part of professional gear.
  • Monitoring headphones and the console's PFL/solo: Closed-back headphones to listen to channels in isolation (solo/PFL) during the line check and soundcheck, without relying on the PA. It lets you check noise, mic and quality of each channel individually.

Step by step

  1. Setup sequence: 1) Study the rider/input list. 2) Define and distribute power with correct grounding. 3) Position and raise the PA covering the audience. 4) Set up the console, stage box and backline. 5) Run and label cables safely (no tripping). 6) Patch according to the input list. 7) Name/color the channels on the console.
  2. Running the line check: Channel by channel: enable phantom where there's a condenser (channel muted), request a signal/generate noise at the source, confirm on the PFL that the right channel receives it without hiss, set an initial gain. Mark the OK channels. Note pending issues (bad cable, faulty microphone) and solve them before soundcheck.
  3. Checking phase and polarity: On pairs (overheads, snare top/bottom, DI+microphone), check whether summing them thickens the sound (in phase) or thins it/loses bass (out of phase). Invert the polarity of the problem channel on the console. Phase solved now avoids a 'hollow' mix later.

In practice: The operator arrives early, sets up from the input list of a 5-piece band, distributes power across two circuits with a UPS for the console, raises the PA aiming at the audience and patches 16 named and color-coded channels. During the line check they find that the tom 2 cable is hissing: they replace it before the band arrives. When the musicians take the stage, everything already works — and the soundcheck becomes fine-tuning, not a hunt for problems.

Summary

  • Professional setup starts from the rider/input list and follows an order: power, PA, cables, patch, names.
  • Well-distributed and grounded power avoids hum and dropouts; clean cabling brings safety and speed.
  • The line check validates each channel and the phase before the band arrives, eliminating most of the scares.

Microphone stands

Each source calls for a type: straight (standing vocals), boom (instruments, cymbal overhead, amplifier), short (kick) and tabletop (lecture). Adjust the height to the source. Tighten the knobs (clutch) and use a counterweight on the boom so it doesn't fall.

Practice in the simulator below.

1d8ea582-14c0-440b-a054-212a9dc25b26.conteudo_rico:
To start: Capture is where quality is born. This lesson is the step-by-step of miking drums, voice and the main instruments, with techniques, positioning and the right microphones for each source.

Positioning the right microphone in the right spot determines the tone before any EQ. Faithful to the base material and enriched with stage practice, this lesson covers drums piece by piece, live voice, guitar (amp and acoustic), bass, keyboard and stereo techniques.

  • Drums — kick and snare: Kick: a specific dynamic microphone (Beta 52A, D112) inside the kick, close to the beater for the 'click', pulled back for more weight. Snare: an SM57 above, aimed at the center/rim; optional microphone on the bottom head (watch the phase). Later EQ and gate shape the attack and cut the bleed.
  • Drums — toms, overheads and hi-hat: Toms: one microphone per tom (a clip-on e604), aimed at the center, close to the head. Overheads: a pair of condensers (C451, AT2021) above the kit in a stereo technique (XY or spaced) to capture the cymbals and the overall image — mind the phase and keep an equal distance to the snare. Hi-hat: a small condenser if needed.
  • Live voice: A cardioid dynamic (SM58/Beta 58A/e835) close to the mouth, using the proximity effect to add body, with the rear aimed at the monitors (anti-feedback). The singer's technique (distance, angle) affects everything; guide the artist to keep a consistent distance. Pop filter in the studio; on stage, the foam and technique control plosives.
  • Guitar and acoustic guitar: Guitar amplifier: an SM57 next to the speaker, slightly off the center of the cone (more cone = more bass/dark; more edge = more brightness/treble); experiment with the position. Live acoustic guitar: a piezo pickup via DI is practical and controls feedback; in a controlled environment, a condenser aimed between the sound hole and the start of the neck (12–18" / ~30–45 cm).
  • Bass, keyboard and stereo techniques: Bass: a DI (clean, direct signal) is the live standard, optionally summed with a microphone on the amplifier. Keyboard: a line output via DI (stereo if applicable). Stereo techniques (XY: two coincident microphones at 90°; ORTF; spaced AB) capture image and depth on overheads, acoustic guitar and ambience — always mindful of phase coherence.

Equipment / details

  • Drum miking kit (Shure/Sennheiser drum kits): Typical sets: Beta 52A/D112 (kick), SM57 (snare), e604 (toms), a pair of condensers (overheads). Clips and short stands. It's the 'arsenal' that handles most live drum kits.
  • Active DI for bass/keyboard and SM57 for amplifiers: Active DI (J48, ProDI) for bass and keyboard; a universal SM57 for the guitar amplifier and snare. With these three capture types the operator can mike almost any standard band.

Step by step

  1. Miking a full drum kit: 1) Kick: position it inside, adjust beater vs. weight by moving the microphone. 2) Snare: SM57 above, aim at the center/rim. 3) Toms: one e604 per tom, close to the head. 4) Overheads: a pair of condensers, equal distances to the snare (phase!), XY or spaced technique. 5) Check the phase of all pairs. 6) Apply an HPF on channels with no useful low end (snare, overheads).
  2. Positioning a live voice microphone: 1) Cardioid dynamic close to the mouth. 2) Rear of the microphone toward the monitors. 3) Guide the singer to keep a constant distance/angle. 4) Set the gain on the loudest part (Module 3). 5) HPF ~100 Hz and light compression for tightness.
  3. Capturing a guitar amplifier: 1) SM57 up against the grille, aligned with the cone. 2) At the center = more body/dark; at the edge = more brightness. 3) Move it a few centimeters, listening, until you find the tone. 4) If you use two microphones, mind the phase between them.

In practice: In a rock band, the drums sound 'muddy' and the snare disappears. The operator redoes the miking: adjusts the overhead with equal distances to the snare (fixes phase), repositions the snare SM57 more toward the rim (gains brightness) and moves the kick microphone closer to the beater (recovers the click that cuts through the mix). Without touching the EQ heavily, the drums become defined — proof that correct capture solves things before processing.

Summary

  • The right capture defines the tone before EQ; each source has ideal microphones and positions.
  • Drums require attention to the phase of pairs (overheads) and a position that balances attack and weight.
  • Live voice uses a close cardioid dynamic, rejecting the monitors; bass and keyboard go via DI.

4df76719-32d1-48d4-94eb-d328dc936001.titulo: Soundcheck, PA Mix and Monitor Mix
4df76719-32d1-48d4-94eb-d328dc936001.conteudo_rico:
To start: The soundcheck is where everything comes together: the gain of each channel, the PA mix for the audience and the monitor mixes for the musicians. This lesson is the complete script of a professional soundcheck.

A soundcheck has a method: with the line check done, you adjust the gain per channel, build the musicians' monitors, then the PA mix — drums as the base, voice on top — and finally the details. This lesson teaches you to run a soundcheck with confidence and good timing.

  • Order of the soundcheck: With the line check done: 1) gain staging of each channel (source at real volume); 2) treat basic dynamics/EQ per channel; 3) build the monitors (each musician hearing the essentials); 4) build the PA mix piece by piece; 5) play a whole song and adjust in context. Order avoids rework.
  • Building the PA mix: Start with the rhythmic base: kick and snare, then the rest of the drums, bass (the low 'bed'), harmonic instruments (guitar, keyboard) and, on top, the voice — which should be intelligible and up front. Use EQ to create space (Module 3.2) and dynamics for tightness. Balance, not 'everything cranked'.
  • Monitor mix: Each musician has different needs: the singer wants voice and a harmonic reference; the drummer wants kick, bass and voice. Use a pre-fader aux (Module 3.4). Rule of thumb: less is more — fill the monitor only with the essentials to reduce bleed and feedback. On IEM, you can do richer mixes safely.
  • Communication with the musicians: Clear and respectful language: 'play just the kick', 'sing the chorus at show volume'. Write down the requests. The operator runs the soundcheck with confidence so as not to waste time. Good communication is as important as the technique.
  • From soundcheck to show: Save the scene at the end of the soundcheck. Remember that an empty room sounds different from a full one (the audience absorbs treble and bass) — anticipate small adjustments as it fills up. Have the base scene ready and monitor the show actively, adjusting faders and dynamics as the music demands.

Equipment / details

  • Digital console with 'sends on faders' and scenes: A feature that turns the faders into send levels for the selected aux, speeding up building monitors. Save the soundcheck scene to recall it at the show. Using a tablet to adjust monitors while walking the stage is common practice.
  • Talkback system and operator's headphones: A talkback microphone to talk to the stage through the monitors and headphones for PFL/solo. Communication and isolated listening speed up the soundcheck.

Step by step

  1. Running the soundcheck (script): 1) Confirm the line check. 2) Ask for each source in isolation and set the gain at the real show volume. 3) Basic EQ/dynamics per channel. 4) Build each monitor (pre-fader aux), starting with each musician's main need. 5) Build the PA mix: drums→bass→harmony→voice. 6) Play a whole song; adjust in context. 7) Save the scene.
  2. Handling monitor requests without causing feedback: Raise only what the musician needs; if they ask for 'more voice' and feedback appears, instead of continuing to raise it, attack the feedback frequency on the monitor's GEQ and check the microphone's aim. Balance between monitor level and stability.
  3. Preparing the transition to the show: Save the scene, have the band and vocal DCAs ready, lower the overall volume for the opening and raise it when the show starts. During the show, follow the dynamics of the songs and the audience's interaction, adjusting actively.

In practice: In a soundcheck with little time before doors open, the operator runs it confidently: line check already done, sets gains in 10 minutes, builds the four monitors prioritizing each musician's essentials, builds the PA mix from drums to voice and runs one song. Saves the scene. When the venue fills up, they raise the voice's treble slightly and adjust the sub, because the audience absorbed part of the highs and lows. Method and anticipation delivered a show on time and sounding good.

Summary

  • The soundcheck follows an order: gain, basic processing, monitors, PA mix and one song in context.
  • The PA mix is built from the rhythmic base to the voice, with EQ creating space; monitors with pre-fader aux and the essentials.
  • Save the scene and anticipate that a full room sounds different from an empty one, adjusting actively during the show.

Monitors: floor monitor (wedge) vs. in-ear monitor (IEM)

Floor monitor (wedge): a speaker on the floor aimed at the musician. Simple, and the musician feels the sound. Risk: feedback and high stage volume. Do the ring out (cut the frequencies that whistle) to gain level safely. Examples: dBTechnologies, L-Acoustics X15, QSC, Yamaha.

In-ear monitor (In-Ear / IEM): a wireless in-ear earphone (transmitter + bodypack). No feedback, a clean stage, a stereo mix and it protects hearing. It needs an ambience mic (otherwise the musician feels isolated) and a limiter (ear protection). Systems: Shure PSM 300/900/1000, Sennheiser EW IEM G4; custom molds (Ultimate Ears, etc.).

The monitor mix is done via aux/send — each musician has THEIR mix, with what THEY asked for (e.g., the vocalist wants the voice loud and the rest in the background). Pre-fade so the monitor doesn't change with the PA fader.

Practice in the simulator below: build the monitor the musician asked for, on the wedge and on the IEM.

28284b0f-a2fd-4621-b6c4-cb3556bc9135.titulo: Fighting Feedback and Operating During the Show
28284b0f-a2fd-4621-b6c4-cb3556bc9135.conteudo_rico:
To start: Feedback is the nightmare of live sound. This lesson teaches you to understand, prevent and eliminate feedback with a method — and to operate with confidence during the show, keeping control from start to finish.

Feedback happens when the sound from the PA returns to the microphone, is re-amplified and creates a loop that grows until it whistles. Fighting feedback means understanding gain before feedback and acting on the causes: positioning, polar pattern, EQ and levels. This lesson also covers active operation during the show.

  • What it is and why it happens: Feedback is a loop: the microphone picks up the sound from the speaker/monitor, the console re-amplifies it, it comes out louder, returns to the microphone... until it oscillates at a frequency (whistle/howl). It happens when the total gain of the loop exceeds unity. The more gain you need, the closer to feedback you operate — hence the importance of efficient capture.
  • Prevention: the causes: Positioning: microphone far from and off the axis of the speakers/monitors; using the cardioid's rear rejection. A suitable polar pattern (super/hypercardioid rejects more). A short source-to-mic distance (more useful signal, less gain needed). Fewer open microphones (gate/mute what isn't in use). Monitors with only the essentials. PA aimed at the audience, not the stage.
  • EQ against feedback: Every room/monitor has frequencies that feed back first. With a graphic EQ (or surgical parametric), identify and attenuate those frequencies before they reach the whistle. Ring out: raise the gain until the feedback starts to sound, identify the frequency and cut it; repeat to gain more 'gain before feedback'. Don't overdo it, so you don't ruin the sound.
  • Tools and suppressors: There are automatic feedback suppressors (on the DriveRack and on some consoles) that detect and cut the frequency. Useful as an aid, but they don't replace good capture and positioning. The competent operator solves the cause; the automatic one covers the residue.
  • Operating during the show: The show is dynamic: follow each song adjusting the voice fader on the verses/choruses, holding the dynamics with the compressor, bringing up a solo, controlling DCAs. Stay alert to surprises (a microphone falling, a cable failing) and have a plan B (a spare channel, an extra microphone). Monitor the PA by walking the area when possible.

Equipment / details

  • Monitor graphic EQ / feedback suppressor (dbx DriveRack): A 31-band GEQ per monitor for surgical cuts of the feedback frequencies; automatic suppressors as support. The 'ring out' of the monitors before the show gains volume margin without whistling.
  • Directional microphones (super/hypercardioid) and gates: More directional microphones (Beta 58A, e935) and gates/mutes on unused channels reduce unnecessary gain in the system, attacking the root cause of feedback.

Step by step

  1. Ringing out a monitor: 1) Slowly raise the monitor level with a microphone open in the usage position. 2) At the threshold of the whistle, identify the frequency (by ear or RTA). 3) Cut that band on the monitor's GEQ. 4) Repeat: another frequency will appear; cut it too. 5) Stop when you have enough volume with a safe margin. Don't cut too much.
  2. Eliminating feedback in the middle of the show: 1) Quickly identify the frequency (low=howl, high=whistle). 2) Slightly lower the guilty channel/monitor. 3) Cut the frequency on the EQ. 4) Check whether a microphone moved out of position or someone aimed it at the monitor. Act calmly and precisely — panic makes it worse.
  3. Actively operating the show mix: Follow the structure of the songs: bring up the voice in the sung parts, open the solo at the right moment, control the dynamics with compression, use DCAs for transitions. Keep an eye on the meters (no clip) and an ear on the room.

In practice: In the middle of a service, a high-pitched whistle appears when the preacher gets close to the speaker.
